meta.scene_summary: We are busy building the future on top of fifteen-year-old bugs and business models that expire before the milk.

cooking.headline: Apple is suing OpenAI because apparently, it's only 'innovation' when they do it.
cooking.summary: Apple is accusing OpenAI of stealing trade secrets, which is a bold move for a company that practically invented the 'we definitely didn't see your feature request' corporate strategy. It's the tech equivalent of a student suing their classmate for copying the homework they both just scraped off Wikipedia.
cooking.plain_english: Apple has filed a lawsuit against OpenAI, alleging that former employees took confidential company information and trade secrets when they moved to the AI startup.

cooking.headline: The EU is officially trying to nerf Meta's infinite dopamine loop.
cooking.summary: The European Union is threatening massive fines if Meta doesn't let users turn off the infinite scroll and autoplay features that keep us mindlessly doomscrolling. It's the digital equivalent of a parent telling you to stop eating candy before dinner or you're getting no allowance.
cooking.plain_english: The European Union is requiring Meta to offer options to disable infinite scrolling and autoplay videos to comply with new regulations.

cooking.headline: SpaceX wants to turn low earth orbit into a giant cloud server
cooking.summary: Elon Musk is planning to launch 100,000 more satellites, because apparently the sky wasn't crowded enough already. It's the ultimate 'move fast and break things' strategy, assuming the thing you're breaking is the view of the stars.
cooking.plain_english: SpaceX has requested permission to launch an additional 100,000 satellites to significantly increase internet bandwidth capacity.

cooking.headline: NYC decides that 'cancel anytime' shouldn't require a master's degree
cooking.summary: New York City is finally banning those deceptive subscription traps that make cancelling harder than a LeetCode hard problem. It's about time we stopped needing to call a retention specialist just to stop paying for a service we haven't used since 2022.
cooking.plain_english: New York City is passing new laws to prevent companies from using confusing or difficult methods to keep customers trapped in unwanted subscription services.

cooking.headline: Automatic C++ to Rust migration is the ultimate 'it works on my machine' fix
cooking.summary: Someone built a tool to automatically translate C++ into memory-safe Rust, which is basically the coding equivalent of asking a psychic to rewrite your legacy spaghetti code. It's a bold attempt to save us from ourselves, provided the compiler doesn't have a nervous breakdown during the process.
cooking.plain_english: A new software tool has been released that automatically converts C++ code into the Rust programming language to improve memory safety.

cooking.headline: A rare victory for the developer vs. the HR department
cooking.summary: Atlassian actually lost a wrongful termination case, proving that even corporate legal teams can't always delete an employee like a buggy PR. It turns out that 'cultural fit' isn't a valid sudo command for firing people without cause.
cooking.plain_english: An Australian court ruled that a software engineer at Atlassian was unfairly fired, marking a significant legal win for an employee in the tech industry.

cooking.headline: China is speedrunning open-source diplomacy with AI
cooking.summary: China is releasing powerful open-source AI models to win global influence, essentially giving the world free homework answers. It turns out the best way to get people to use your tech stack is to stop gatekeeping it.
cooking.plain_english: China is increasing its international influence by releasing high-performing open-source artificial intelligence models for global use.

cooked.headline: GhostLock has been sitting in Linux for 15 years, just waiting for its moment.
cooked.summary: Finding a bug that has been hiding in plain sight since 2010 is the ultimate reminder that we are all just building castles on top of very shaky, very old Legos. It turns out the 'it works on my machine' philosophy works even better when you don't check the kernel code for a decade and a half.
cooked.plain_english: A vulnerability that allows unauthorized access to computer memory has been discovered in all versions of the Linux operating system released over the last fifteen years.

cooked.headline: Meta's AI image feature had a shorter shelf life than my last milk carton.
cooked.summary: Meta rushed out an AI image tool only to pull the plug once users started testing the guardrails. It turns out that 'move fast and break things' is a terrible strategy when your product starts generating content that shouldn't exist.
cooked.plain_english: Meta has removed a new AI image generation feature following negative feedback from users.

cooked.headline: Companies are realizing that burning VC cash on API calls isn't a business model
cooked.summary: Businesses spent the last year throwing money at AI like it was a magic vending machine that prints profit. Now they're realizing that paying for expensive tokens to write their emails is just a very fancy way to set money on fire.
cooked.plain_english: Companies are looking for ways to reduce the high costs associated with using artificial intelligence services.
